LAW OFFICES OF

O'MELVENY & MYERS
611 WEST SIXTH STREET
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90017
TELEPHONE (213) 620-1120
TELEX 67-4122
CABLE ADDRESS "MOMS"

1800 CENTURY PARK EAST
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90067
TELEPHONE (213) 553-6700
TELEX 67-4097
1800 M STREET, N.W.
WASHINGTON, D.C. 20036
TELEPHONE (202) 383-5300
TELEX 89-622
4 PLACE DE LA CONCORDE
PARIS 8e FRANCE
TELEPHONE 265 39-33
TELEX 842-660/15

OUR FILE NUMBER

302,145-105

February
2nd
1979

Mr. John Giansello
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California

Re General Motors Corporation

Dear John:

Thank you for calling Wednesday to respond to
the questions raised in our meeting on January 18th.
This letter summarizes my understanding of our discussion,
which related to the application of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act") to "CIP/GM," the political action
committee sponsored by General Motors Corporation ("GM").

We had earlier discussed the method CIP/GM
should use to compute which persons must be reported as
donors on the campaign statements filed pursuant to the
Act. The Act requires reporting of donors who give $100
or more which is attributable to California collections.
In the case of committees such as CIP/GM, such reporting
is required only if the "California portion" of a donor's
contributions equals or exceeds $100 on a cumulative
basis. In our meeting, we had discussed the proper way
to determine the "California portion" and the period for
which it is to be computed.

Your response after considering the matter with
other staff members was that an initial, good faith
estimate of the percentage of nondesignated funds to be
expended in connection with California state and local
elections should be made. This percentage figure should

#2 - Mr. John Giansello - 2/2/79

be used throughout a cumulation period unless actual
experience leads to the conclusion that a higher or
lower percentage is appropriate. Temporary fluctua-
tions in patterns of expenditures which cause the actual
ratio of "California" to "non-California" contributions
to exceed the estimate will not require an adjustment in
the estimated percentage unless they cause the persons
filing CIP/GM's reports to believe the estimate will be
inaccurate throughout the cumulation period.

The good faith estimate should be applied
only to undesignated contributions. Contributions
designated by the donor for California state or local
use should be itemized if they equal or exceed $100.
When CIP/GM forwards the designated contributions, the
check sent should be either be that of the donor or a
check that identifies CIP/GM. If a cashier's check or
a check which merely identifies the fiduciary used by
GM to administer the CIP/GM funds, the fiduciary would
run the risk of being deemed a "committee" under the
Act. Designated contributions must be accompanied by a
notice or letter which identifies the donor, gives the
donor's address and identifies the donor's occupation
or employer, even if the designated contribution is less
than $100.

CIP/GM should report as receipts all designated
contributions (itemizing those from individuals whose
aggregate California contributions equal or exceed $100),
and all "California portions" of undesignated gifts which
exceed or equal $100 on a cumulative basis. In addition,
the difference between the amounts spent in California
state and local elections and the amount of (1) designated
contributions plus (2) itemized nondesignated contributions
should be reported as "received" for California purposes.
It is not necessary to report an amount equal to the
"California portion" of all undesignated contributions as
received in California.

You indicated that CIP/GM would not be regarded
by the staff as complying with the Act if it filed a
schedule of all persons who donated $100 or more, even if
the schedule were accompanied by a disclosure of the
"California portion" percentage that would apply to each
contribution.

#3 - Mr. John Giansello - 2/2/79

Because the method outlined above should result
in equal receipts and expenditures in California for each
report, CIP/GM should never have a "surplus" of funds
deemed "allocated" to California under normal circumstances.
If, however, a large number of donors gave an amount which
caused their "California portion" to equal or exceed $100
and CIP/GM had not made contributions equal to the sum of
the "California portions" reported as received from those
persons, such a surplus could result. In that case, you
indicated that an "expenditure" described as a "transfer
of funds from California to general use," or in a similar
fashion, could be reported. It will not be necessary to
trace the "surplus" funds to specific contributions in
other states or in federal elections.

CIP/GM must also report the receipt of adminis­
trative support from GM. With respect to general overhead
expenses incurred and the fees paid to the fiduciary for
handling CIP/GM's funds, the same "California portion"
used to determine whether a donor must be reported should
be used to determine the amount to be reported on Schedule
C of Form 420. Any specific expenses attributable to
participation in California (for instance, a specific
brochure printed for use in California or a specific fee
charged by the fiduciary for filing a California report)
should be separately reported in full. Additionally, the
"California portion" of such expenses as printing and
postage should be reported.

With respect to the salaries of the GM employees
who administer CIP/GM, only the salaries of persons
spending 10% or more of their compensated time in a
calendar month need be considered, and then only for
months in which the "10%" test is met. The "California
portion" of the salaries of these employees may be
reported. Alternatively, if GM's records permit such an
allocation, it would be permissible to report the salary
paid for activities in connection with the California
activities of CIP/GM.

Finally, you confirmed that the Act's restrictions
on lobbyist contributions apply only to persons who engage
in activities which make them "lobbyists" within the
meaning of Section 82039 of the Act. Activities designed
to influence activities of the federal government or states
other than California would not render the persons engaging

#4 - Mr. John Giansello - 2/2/79

in them ineligible to give to CIP/GM.

This description of our discussions has been
somewhat lengthy because a copy is being sent to David
Fischer to confirm my telephone advice to him. Please
contact me if you wish to discuss further any of the
matters outlined above.

Sincerely,

Peter J. Tennyson
for O'MELVENY & MYERS

PJT:gd

cc: David Fischer, Esq.


LAW OFFICES OF

O'MELVENY & MYERS
611 WEST SIXTH STREET
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90017
TELEPHONE (213) 620-1120
TELEX 67-4122
CABLE ADDRESS "MOMS"

1800 CENTURY PARK EAST
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90067
TELEPHONE (213) 553-6700
TELEX 67-4297

1800 M STREET, N.W.
WASHINGTON, D.C. 20036
TELEPHONE (202) 383-5300
TELEX 89-622

4 PLACE DE LA CONCORDE
PARIS 8e, FRANCE
TELEPHONE 266 39-33
TELEX 280-6670

January
25th
1979

OUR FILE NUMBER

302,145-105


Mr. John Giansello
Fair Political Practices
Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California

Re General Motors Corporation

Dear John:

Thank you for taking the time to meet with David
Fisher, Max Messmer, Lee Ridgeway and myself last week
concerning General Motors Corporation ("GM"). If I recall
correctly, you were going to consider further several
matters and get in touch with David or me next week or
shortly thereafter. The matters that you were still
considering included the matters discussed below. The
questions involved proposed activities of GM's "political
action committee," which is named "Civic Involvement
Program/General Motors" ("CIP/GM").

Can a federally registered political action com-
mittee which has reported certain sums as "received" for
California purposes but has decided not to expend such sums
in California report an expenditure or disbursement on its
campaign statement (presumably its final campaign statement
for a period) which indicates that the balance in the
"California" account has been transferred to the committee's
general funds, or must the balance be traced to actual con-
tributions elsewhere?

CIP/GM recognizes, of course, that all contribu-
tions which are designated or "earmarked" for use in
California elections should be reported on its California
campaign statements if it permits such designation and
becomes a "committee" within the meaning of the Political

#2 - Mr. John Giansello - 1/25/79

Reform Act.  In addition to reporting persons whose actual
designated contributions equal or exceed $100, CIP/GM would
plan to report on a non-itemized basis some portion of the
undesignated funds it receives.  The total amount reported
as received would in all cases be at least equal to the
amount expended in connection with California state and
local elections.  In determining the amount to report as
received, and in determining whether a person who makes non-
designated contributions must be specifically disclosed
as a donor to CIP/GM, we discussed several possible methods:

     (1)  If it can be determined or estimated in
good faith that the proportion of undesignated
funds used in California to undesignated funds
used in other jurisdictions will be less than a
particular level (the "California portion"), only
persons whose "California portion" of their
contribution equals or exceeds $100 need be
specifically identified in campaign statements
filed by CIP/GM.

     (2)  Mr. Fisher asked that you consider whether
it was necessary to estimate the "California portion"
or whether the reporting obligation could be computed
on the basis of the actual ratio of the amounts spent in
California during the period in question to the
total nationwide expenditures of CIP/GM for the same
period.  Although such a method might provide numbers
some with respect to individual contributors to
CIP/GM, the actual method used might be irrelevant
if the "California portion" remains at a low level,
for instance 5% or less.  In such an instance, it
is unlikely that any undesignated contribution would
required itemized reporting and whether a fixed
estimate is used or whether an actual computation is
made of the "California portion" for the period in
question would not seem particularly significant.
The method used might, however, make a difference
in determining the amount of GM's expenditures in
support of CIP/GM which must be reported by CIP/GM as a
"contribution" in California.

     Under Rule 18419, support expenditures will not
cause GM to become a committee if CIP/GM reports them but
a "California portion" must be computed of such administrative
expenses.  Would it be permissible for CIP/GM to compute
and report only the actual California portion of GM's expenses
for each period as a receipt from GM?  Of course, this
amount reported as "received" in California will also be

#3 - Mr. John Giansello - 1/25/79

reported as "expended" on administrative and support costs.
In connection with such reporting, will itemization of
the expenses be necessary or will it be sufficient to state
that the amount "received" from GM in the form of admin-
istrative support was expended in the same manner?

Another issue which you indicated that you would
consider is whether a schedule of donors listing all persons
who gave $100 or more in undesignated contributions to
CIP/GM would be accepted as complying with the requirements
of the Political Reform Act if the schedule included with
the report provided all information required to be shown
about persons whose donations must be reported under the Act.
Although realizing that if it uses a "percentage" or estimate
approach to determine the "California portion," none of these
individuals may have given amounts which would require dis-
closure of their names, CIP/GM may conclude that for administra-
tive reasons it wishes to report all such persons. The names
of these donors are being disclosed in other reports in any
event. If CIP/GM discloses all persons who give $100 or
more in undesignated funds and provides the other information
required by the Political Reform Act, would such "over-
disclosure" be regarded as a failure to comply with the
requirements of the Act? If so, would a statement accompanying
the schedule concerning the "California portion" of the donations
reported on the schedule resolve any difficulties presented?

Another matter addressed during our meeting was
the subject of contributions by lobbyists. GM understands
that the Political Reform Act prohibits such contributions
with respect to California state and local elections and that
any portion of a contribution from a person who is a "lobbyist"
within the meaning of the Political Reform Act might be
regarded as having been used in California. Accordingly,
CIP/GM will continue its policy of prohibiting those who
engage in lobbying activities in California from contributing
to CIP/GM. Presumably, however, the Political Reform Act's
restriction on contributions by lobbyists applies only to
persons who are lobbyists within the meaning of the Act. On
this basis, unless you indicate that the Commission or the
staff has taken a contrary position, GM would propose not
to prohibit persons who engage in lobbying activities in
states other than California (but do not engage in activities
which would make them "lobbyists" within the meaning of
Section 82039 of the Act) from contributing to CIP/GM. Of
course, CIP/GM would investigate the laws of the states in
which the individuals concerned engage in lobbying to see
if those states have restrictions similar to those in the
Political Reform Act.

#4 - Mr. John Giansello - 1/25/79

David Fisher indicated to us that when CIP/GM
receives a "designated" contribution, the contribution is
generally designated for a specific candidate or ballot
measure. In such an event, is CIP/GM an "intermediary"
or is the depository who receives the contribution and
forwards it on behalf of the person making the designated
contribution the "intermediary" who must report as such
pursuant to the Political Reform Act? Obviously, since
CIP/GM would already be filing reports pursuant to the Act,
it would be more convenient for it to report as the
"intermediary" delivering the contributions. We believe it
would also be more appropriate in view of the purposes of
the Act, since one report would then include all funds
contributed with respect to California state and local
elections by persons connected with or affiliated with GM.

We appreciate the fact that your time is limited
and that some of the issues we discussed at our meeting
and which have been raised again in this letter are complex.
If further discussions seem appropriate before you feel in
a position to reply, please contact the undersigned at
(213)620-1120 or Mr. Fisher in Detroit for additional
information.

Thank you again for taking time to meet with us
and to assist in resolving these issues.

Sincerely,

Peter J. Tennyson
for O'MELVENY & MYERS

PJT:cy

sections.question_synthetic: The letter requests FPPC guidance on how General Motors' CIP/GM political action committee should compute and report California-attributable contributions and expenses under the Political Reform Act, including methods for calculating the "California portion" of undesignated funds and whether transfers of undesignated surplus funds require tracing to specific contributions.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: This is the incoming request letter dated 1/25/79, not an FPPC response. A follow-up letter dated 2/2/79 from the same law office appears to be a confirmation letter summarizing FPPC's telephone advice, but the primary document is the initial inquiry. The document contains extensive detailed questions but no formal CONCLUSION section from FPPC. OCR formatting affects some text clarity.

We had earlier discussed the method CIP/GM should use to compute which persons must be reported as donors on the campaign statements filed pursuant to the Act. The Act requires reporting of donors who give $100 or more which is attributable to California collections. In the case of committees such as CIP/GM, such reporting is required only if the "California portion" of a donor's contributions equals or exceeds $100 on a cumulative basis. In our meeting, we had discussed the proper way to determine the "California portion" and the period for which it is to be computed. Your response after considering the matter with other staff members was that an initial, good faith estimate of the percentage of nondesignated funds to be expended in connection with California state and local elections should be made. This percentage figure should #2 - Mr. John Giansello - 2/2/79 be used throughout a cumulation period unless actual experience leads to the conclusion that a higher or lower percentage is appropriate. Temporary fluctua- tions in patterns of expenditures which cause the actual ratio of "California" to "non-California" contributions to exceed the estimate will not require an adjustment in the estimated percentage unless they cause the persons filing CIP/GM's reports to believe the estimate will be inaccurate throughout the cumulation period. The good faith estimate should be applied only to undesignated contributions. Contributions designated by the donor for California state or local use should be itemized if they equal or exceed $100. When CIP/GM forwards the designated contributions, the check sent should be either be that of the donor or a check that identifies CIP/GM. If a cashier's check or a check which merely identifies the fiduciary used by GM to administer the CIP/GM funds, the fiduciary would run the risk of being deemed a "committee" under the Act. Designated contributions must be accompanied by a notice or letter which identifies the donor, gives the donor's address and identifies the donor's occupation or employer, even if the designated contribution is less than $100. CIP/GM should report as receipts all designated contributions (itemizing those from individuals whose aggregate California contributions equal or exceed $100), and all "California portions" of undesignated gifts which exceed
embedding.summary: O'Melveny & Myers law firm sends an incoming request letter to the FPPC on behalf of General Motors Corporation regarding compliance issues for its political action committee (CIP/GM) under the Political Reform Act, seeking clarification on donor reporting thresholds, contribution allocation methods, and expense reporting.
